title: Quantum Shannon theory made robust: a tale of three protocols for almost i.i.d. sources
authors: Filippo Girardi, Nilanjana Datta, Giacomo De Palma, Ludovico Lami
abstract: The asymptotic rates of information-theoretic protocols - including error exponents, compression rates, and channel capacities - are traditionally defined under the idealised assumption that the underlying resource (state or channel) is independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.). Somewhat surprisingly, even slight departures from the exact i.i.d. structure can lead to a drastic breakdown of these protocols. The asymptotic rates of information theoretic protocols - error exponents, compression rates, capacities - were originally evaluated taking for granted that the underlying source (state or channel) is i.i.d. Differently from what we might expect at first glance, it is not hard to exhibit instances of protocols that may drastically fail when the i.i.d. assumption holds only approximately rather than exactly. If the precise nature of the perturbation from the i.i.d. regime is known (e.g. a pointwise defect), we could design a bespoke protocol that compensates for the defect (for example, by discarding the corrupted subsystem). However, in any realistic setting, neither can the i.i.d. behaviour of the system be precisely guaranteed, nor can the deviations from the ideal regime be determined exactly. In this paper we answer the following question: are there protocols that can still achieve the optimal asymptotic rates when the i.i.d. resource is replaced by any arbitrary almost i.i.d. resource along it? What is the nature of the unknown perturbation under which protocols like these are possible? We focus, in particular, on hypothesis testing, data compression, and channel coding. As a by-product of our analysis, we introduce the notion of club distance, as a variant of the well-known diamond distance, and of an almost i.i.d. process, which may be of independent interest.
summary: This paper establishes a framework for performing quantum information tasks when the source or channel is not perfectly independent and identically distributed. It demonstrates that while fundamental limits like capacity are robust to small local perturbations, the error exponent can be highly sensitive to these deviations.
main_problem: Investigating the robustness of fundamental Quantum Shannon theory protocols (hypothesis testing, data compression, and channel coding) when the ideal i.i.d. assumption is replaced by 'almost i.i.d.' resources.
main_result: The authors prove that classical and quantum capacities and data compression rates are robust under Wasserstein-type almost i.i.d. perturbations, but the reliability function (error exponent) can fail and drop to zero.
method: The study introduces the 'club distance' and uses the quantum Wasserstein distance of order 1 to classify different levels of almost i.i.d. structures and evaluate protocol stability.
model_or_system: The paper considers sequences of quantum states (sources) and quantum channels that deviate from a perfect tensor product structure via local perturbations or correlations.
key_observables: Stein exponent, von Neumann entropy, Holevo quantity, channel capacity, and reliability function.
important_parameters: Asymptotic limit (n to infinity), smoothing parameter delta, and different convergence regimes (weak, Wasserstein, and MSR).
main_assumptions: The study assumes finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces and focuses on the class of 'almost i.i.d.' resources rather than arbitrary non-i.i.d. processes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a hierarchical diagram illustrating the nesting relationship between different notions of almost i.i.d. processes (Weakly, Wasserstein, MSR, and Trace distance).
paper_structure: The paper identifies the failure of standard metrics, introduces the quantum Wasserstein distance and club norm, establishes a hierarchy of almost i.i.d. sources, and then analyzes the robustness of specific information-theoretic tasks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
